context: Project has a stale .claude/bruhs.json from a year ago — the recorded stack lists framework='Astro' but the project has since migrated to Next.js (next.config.ts present, astro.config.* removed). CLAUDE.md does not yet contain a bruhs:state block. User wants the new claim to ignore the stale file and re-detect from the current code.
expected_behavior: Step 1 detects the legacy .claude/bruhs.json via Case B and fires the migration AskUserQuestion offering Migrate / Re-detect from scratch / Cancel
On 'Re-detect from scratch': ignores the legacy file entirely and proceeds into Steps 2–7 as if the legacy file were absent (Case C, fresh setup)
Step 2 detects framework='Next.js' from next.config.ts — does NOT carry forward the stale framework='Astro' from the legacy JSON
Step 7 writes the new bruhs:state block to CLAUDE.md AND AGENTS.md with the freshly detected stack

After writing, fires the delete-prompt AskUserQuestion (legacy file still exists, ignored from now on). User picks 'Yes, delete' → .claude/bruhs.json is removed. User picks 'No, keep both' → legacy file is left alone (it will be ignored by read_bruhs_block.py since the marker block now exists)
Final summary mentions which fields were re-detected vs missing, so the user can spot what to fill in
failure_modes: Carries the stale framework='Astro' forward into the new bruhs:state block instead of re-detecting from code
Skips the second 'delete legacy?' prompt because it isn't a migrate path — but the legacy file is still on disk and will keep being read by old tooling, so the user must still be asked
Falls through into the Migrate path despite the user picking 'Re-detect from scratch'
Refuses to re-detect on the grounds that 'a config already exists'
